summary: Gruber argues that the term 'cursor' is overloaded in computing, referring to both the mouse/trackpad pointer and the text insertion point. He notes that Apple's marketing materials for iPadOS 13.4 trackpad support use 'cursor' to describe the pointer, while Apple's own technical documentation and Human Interface Guidelines correctly use 'pointer.' He recommends using 'pointer' and 'insertion point' for clarity, and highlights Apple's own Style Guide as the authority on precise terminology.
key_points: The word 'cursor' is ambiguous because it refers to both the mouse pointer and the text insertion point — two fundamentally different UI elements.
Apple's marketing materials for iPadOS 13.4 use 'cursor' while their technical Human Interface Guidelines correctly use 'pointer,' showing an internal inconsistency.
The terminal/command-line is the one context where 'cursor' is the proper term for the blinking insertion point.
Apple's own Style Guide recommends being specific about pointer types: arrow, crosshair, I-beam.
money_quotes: Cursor is an overloaded term. There are two discrete elements of modern computing that we loosely refer to as 'cursors'.
For clarity, it's best not to refer to either of these things as cursors. Instead: Mouse/trackpad pointer. Insertion point.
But as a general rule, it's better to err on the side of precision, and pointer and insertion point always avoid ambiguity.
And, of course, the Apple Style Guide prescribes OK, never okay.
key_insight: Precise terminology matters in computing — 'pointer' and 'insertion point' should replace the ambiguous catch-all 'cursor' even though Apple's own marketing doesn't always follow this rule.
